EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracking along the east coast brought a wintry mix of precipitation to the region. Ice accretion was one quarter of an inch in most locations with a few isolated locations in Rockingham Co. receiving one third inch accretion.  Snowfall was a secondary element with 3 to 4 inches being the norm.  The exception was Ashe Co. where snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 5 inches.

A persistent southeast fetch of moisture laden air encountered the eastern slopes of the Blue Ridge. This scenario allowed for numerous showers and thunderstorms to produce copious rainfall in a very localized region of North Carolina -- all areas of steep terrain.  The resultant run-off produced areas of flash flooding and subsequent flooding during the evening of the 13th and the early morning hours of the 14th.  Specific flash flooding in Watuaga Co. included flooding near the Boone Mall when Kraut Creek left its banks.  This flash flooding resulted in the need for one swift water rescue.  In Boone, flash flooding along the Kraut Creek and South Fork of the New River prompted flash flooding on several roads in town.  Because of this, Boone police discouraged travel.  In Surry Co., flash flooding along the Lovills Creek left its banks and caused flash flooding in the town of Mount Airy.  Some streets had 2 to 3 feet of water covering them.  Some homes were also flooded in Mount Airy due to water flowing down a street.

After light snow fell for much of the overnight hours across the foothills, the precipitation began to increase in intensity, while mixing with sleet by around sunrise. By late morning, precipitation changed to mainly sleet, which then changed to freezing rain before ending around midnight. While snow accumulations totaled around 2 inches across the area, the 1/4 to 1/2 inch of sleet and ice that accumulated on top of the snow created extremely hazardous driving conditions. Numerous traffic accidents resulted.

Light snow fell across the mountains during the evening hours. Significant accumulations were mainly confined to the ridgetops and high valleys near the Tennessee border where 1 to 3 inch totals were common. However, a dusting was observed as far south and east as Waynesville and the higher elevations of eastern Buncombe County. Roads became very slick and hazardous, and there were several traffic accidents, particularly in the Newland and Banner Elk areas.

A low pressure system spread moist air above a cold air mass in place at the surface across southwest North Carolina creating a mixture of freezing rain and sleet over the region.  Much of the area ended up with ice accumulation around one quarter inch.  The weight of the ice downed trees and power lines.

Heavy snow fell during the overnight hours across the northern mountains. Occasional heavy snow and blowing snow caused blizzard-like conditions at times, especially in Avery County. Snowfall ranged from 2 to 4 inches in the valleys an 4 to 6 inches in the higher elevations. As wet roads froze underneath the snow, they became quite slick and hazardous, causing quite a few accidents. In addition, temperatures in the single digits combined with strong winds to produce wind chills of 10 below.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light sleet, occasionally mixed with snow, fell for much of the early morning hours across the piedmont. The precipitation began to increase in intensity during mid-to-late morning. Precipitation began to change over to freezing rain during the afternoon, and this continued into the evening hours. Up to an inch of snow fell across the area during the morning, but sleet and ice made the greatest impact. Most locations received between 1/2 to 1 inch of sleet. In addition, freezing rain deposited a glaze of ice over the sleet during the afternoon and evening, creating extremely dangerous driving conditions, and numerous accidents.

A couple  specific reports include a plate glass window being blown out an automobile parts store in West Jefferson, and trees down near the Blue Ridge Parkway in Glade Valley.  Wind gusts were measured at 54 kts at the aiport in Boone.

Light snow fell across the southern and central mountains overnight. The heaviest accumulations occurred near the Tennessee border, where 1-2 inch totals were common.  Some of the higher elevations received as much as 4 inches. Accumulations tapered off rapidly toward the east, as locations from Franklin to Waynesville to Asheville received only a dusting. Roads became very slick and hazardous, and there were several traffic accidents, especially on I-40 through Haywood County.

One half inch to one and one half inches of snow fell across northeast North Carolina. The snow produced slippery roadways, which resulted in several accidents. The highest amounts were reported at Jackson in Northampton county 1.5", Lewiston in Bertie county 1.5", and Murfreesboro in Hertford county 1".

Freezing drizzle and fog deposited a light accretion of ice on some road surfaces across the northern mountains and foothills. Most of the slick roads were along the Blue Ridge, including the Jonas Ridge area, and highway 421 into Watauga County. The situation worsened slightly during the daylight hours, as the drizzle turned to light rain.  Several traffic accidents occurred.

A line of severe thunderstorms moved through much of North Carolina during the morning and early afternoon hours of March 8th. In general, Eastern North Carolina experienced straight-line wind gusts from 50 to 90 mph. A wind gust of 110 mph was recorded at Kill Devil Hills on the Outer Banks of Dare County where significant structural damage occurred, including the destruction of a communications tower. Sporadic to widespread wind damage occurred across the entire area with numerous trees and power lines down, and minor to significant structural damage. There was one injury in Hyde County when a mobile home was overturned and demolished by the fierce winds.

A quarter inch of ice accrued in the central and western Piedmont, which created icy roads and caused numerous accidents.

Light snow fell across the southern and central mountains overnight. The heaviest accumulations occurred near the Tennessee border, where 1-2 inch totals were common.  Some of the higher elevations received as much as 4 inches. Accumulations tapered off rapidly toward the east, as locations from Franklin to Waynesville to Asheville received only a dusting. Roads became very slick and hazardous, and there were several traffic accidents, especially on I-40 through Haywood County.

Light snow, mixed with sleet, fell for much of the morning hours across the piedmont. The precipitation began to increase in intensity as it changed mainly to sleet and freezing rain during the afternoon. After 1 to 3 inches of snow fell across the area during the morning and early afternoon, a layer of 1/4 to 1/2 inch of sleet, as well as a thin layer of glaze, accumulated on top of the snow during the afternoon and evening.  This created extremely dangerous driving conditions and numerous accidents.

A line of severe thunderstorms moved through much of North Carolina during the morning and early afternoon hours of March 8th. In general, Eastern North Carolina experienced straight-line wind gusts from 50 to 90 mph. A wind gust of 110 mph was recorded at Kill Devil Hills on the Outer Banks of Dare County where significant structural damage occurred, including the destruction of a communications tower. Sporadic to widespread wind damage occurred across the entire area with numerous trees and power lines down, and minor to significant structural damage. There was one injury in Hyde County when a mobile home was overturned and demolished by the fierce winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved across the southeastern U.S., then up the east coast during the 27th and 28th of February. Heavy snow fell across the foothills and mountains of northwest North Carolina during the evening of the 27th and through the 28th. Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 6 inches in the foothills, and 7 to 12 inches across the mountains.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: 3.5 to 4 inch elongated hail reported at I-540 and Falls of Neuse Road.  3 inch hail reported at Strickland and Falls of Neuse Roads.  2 inch hail reported in Five Points.  Golf ball sized hail reported at Cameron Village, Atlantic and New Hope Church Roads, North Raleigh Community Hospital, Green and Lee Spring Roads, and Durant and Falls of Neuse Roads.  Ping pong ball sized hail reported on Wake Forest Road.  Quarter to half dollar sized hail reported on Highwoods Road.
Hen egg sized hail reported on Lake Wheeler Road.  Quarter to half dollar sized hail reported in Fuquay Varina and at Highways 401 and 70 in Garner.
Nickel hail at Ray Road and Route 210.
Half dollar sized hail reported in Harris Crossroads near Kerr Lake.

The highest amounts were reported at Jackson in Northampton county 1.5", Lewiston in Bertie county 1.5", and Murfreesboro in Hertford county 1".

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Ellerbe, and at scattered other locations across the county.  The Richmond County AWOS reported a measured gust of 64 mph.
Numerous trees were blown down at the Asheboro Zoo.  A tin roof was torn off of a building in Asheboro, and trees and power lines were downed as well.  Dime sized hail was also reported.

A few trees were blown down in Ansonville and Lilesville.  A porch was ripped of a trailer near Wadesboro, and a roof was torn off a barn.  Further south, in Morven, a roof was partially torn off of a home.
A barn collapsed, injuring two people.
Trees were blown down near Carthage.
Golfball sized hail reported at New Garden and Horsepen creek.

Numerous trees were blown down near Highway 401 north of Laurinburg and on Turnpike Road.  Trees fell on a couple of houses on Anita Drive in town, and trees fell on cars in the parking lot of Scotland Memorial's medical facilities.  A 100-year-old barn on Peabridge Road was destroyed.
The roof of a metal building was partially removed.  A shed and two garages were destroyed on Wright Road.  A tree fell on a roof, and wind ripped apart a storage building on Lake Bay Road.
Power lines were blown down.
Power lines were blown down, and small hail was reported as well.
Numerous trees and power lines were blown down.  A hay barn was destroyed on Julian Road, and across the street a brick tobacco barn was destroyed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A utility building was blown away in town.  Large trees were blown down on Ten Ten Road.  Homes were damaged in the Fairview area, north of Fuquay-Varina, with widespread reports of trees down and power outages.  Six to eight homes were damaged just southwest of Garner, with large pine and oak trees on houses.  Numerous trees were blown down on Senter Farm Road.
Trees and power lines were blown down just west of Youngsville, on  Halifax Road, Pearces Road, and Ferrels Bridge Road.
A tree fell through a mobile home on NC 231.  Near Knightdale, several large limbs were blown down.

Trees and power lines were blown down in town, and a home was destroyed.  Mobile home windows were blown out, injuring a person.  From Autryville to Newton Grove, numerous trees and powerlines were blown down.  Just north of Autryville, in the Midway community, a church was severely damaged.  A couple of  vehicles were flipped, a storage shed was destroyed, and several homes sustained damage as well.  Seven barns and three turkey houses were destroyed.  A hog house on Seed Mill Road sustained severe damage, but only one of the 1200 hogs was lost.
Several trees and power lines were blown down in Nashville, Red Oak, and Bailey.
Two houses were heavily damaged on Heath Glenn Road, and a tree fell through the center of another house on Stantonsburg Road.

The roof was removed from a house on Antioch Road, and a person inside was injured.  On Piedmont Airline Road, another house was heavily damaged, injuring the person inside.  Structural damage was also reported on Patetown Road.  On US 13 South, a few metal shelters were destroyed.  Numerous trees and power lines were blown down.  Strong winds damaged part of the Wayne Country Day School, with one minor injury.  A civilian forecaster at Seymour Johnson Air Force Base reported winds of 61 mph.
An old building sustained roof damage.
A shed was destroyed near I-40 close to the Pender County line.
Trees were blown down and a shed was destroyed.
Trees and power lines were blown down near the Cross Creek Mall.   Pea to dime sized hail was reported as well.
Quarter sized hail reported at Kyland and Dozier Roads.

Trees were blown down at McConnell Church Road in McCleansville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: KFBG METAR reported 66 mph wind gust.  Several homes on Ft. Bragg property sustained roof damage.  Simmons Army Airfield sustained a significant amount of damage to four helicopter hangars, government vehicles, and outdoor structures.

Trees and power lines were blown down on Shotwell Road near US 70.  A tree also fell on a house along Shotwell Road.  A barn was blown down and equipment was damaged on Cornwallis Road.  The old Champion building sustained roof damage.

The roof was destroyed on the old Selma Ice, Coal, and Oil Company.  BB&T, Selma Fire & EMS, McClung's Electrical, and the Selma Police Department all sustained damage.  Windows were blown out at the Wee Tots daycare.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain began falling during the evening of the 2nd across the mountains, and continued overnight before ending by mid-morning of the 3rd. Up to 1/8 inch of ice accumulated, mainly on elevated surfaces. Slick spots developed on roads, and trees fell here and there across the area.

Snow showers redeveloped during the evening of the 28th, continuing off and on through the evening of the 1st. Additional snowfall accumulations of 1 to 2 inches were observed. The higher elevations along the Tennessee border were the main areas affected. However, some valley locations as far east as northern Buncombe and northern Jackson counties received light accumulations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate sound-side flooding occurred across northern portions of the Outer Banks near Oregon Inlet during the early morning hours of Sunday, April 3rd. Highway 12 was closed for several hours with one foot of water reported over the road.

A Noreaster affected Eastern North Carolina during the middle of the month resulting in persistent strong wind gusts measured from near 40 mph to slightly over 60 mph. This in combination with large ocean swells resulted in coastal flooding and high surf from north of Hatteras on the ocean side in Dare county, and sound-side flooding along western portions of the Pamlico sound including the Neuse and Trent Rivers, affecting Craven, Pamlico, and Eastern Carteret counties. Water level rises between 3 to 5 feet resulting in many road closures, and some property damage.

Wet snow, mixed with rain and sleet at times, began falling across western North Carolina during the evening of the 27th, and continued into the morning hours of the 28th. Total snow accumulations ranged from a trace across the southwest mountain valleys and lower elevations of the foothills and piedmont south of I-40, to 2 inches in the higher elevations, and in piedmont and foothills locations north of I-40. By around sunrise on the 28th, most of the precipitation had changed to rain.

Snow showers redeveloped during the evening of the 28th, continuing off and on through the afternoon of the 1st, mainly affecting the counties along the Tennessee border. Additional snowfall accumulations of 1 to 2 inches were observed during this time.

Wet snow, mixed with rain and sleet at times, began falling across western North Carolina during the evening of the 27th, and continued into the morning hours of the 28th. Total snow accumulations ranged from a trace across the southwest mountain valleys and lower elevations of the foothills and piedmont south of I-40, to 2 inches in the higher elevations, and in piedmont and foothills locations north of I-40. Isolated heavier totals up to 13 inches occurred along  the Blue Ridge, north of I-40, while the lower elevations of the foothills generally received only 1 to 3 inches.

A Noreaster affected Eastern North Carolina during the middle of the month resulting in persistent strong wind gusts measured from near 40 mph to slightly over 60 mph. This in combination with large ocean swells resulted in coastal flooding and high surf from north of Hatteras on the ocean side in Dare county, and sound-side flooding along western portions of the Pamlico sound including the Neuse and Trent Rivers, affecting Craven, Pamlico, and Eastern Carteret counties. Water level rises between 3 to 5 feet resulting in many road closures, and some property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted from 60 to 63 mph at Alligator River Bridge and Hatteras village between noon and 3 PM.

A late season storm brought significant snowfall to the mountains of North Carolina. Accumulations were highly variable across the area, with as much as 8 inches falling in the higher elevations. However, even locations in the lower French Broad Valley observed up to 3 inches.

Wet snow, heavy at times, developed in the higher elevations of the North Carolina mountains during the morning. By early evening, snowfall amounts ranged from trace amounts to 4 inches, mainly in areas above 3500 feet.  By early evening, some of the highest elevations reeached heavy snowfall criteria, mainly above 4000 feet. Light accumulations were reported in locations as low as 3000 feet. Meanwhile, the major valleys received mostly rain.

High winds developed across the mountains and foothills during the evening, and continued through the overnight hours before subsiding during the late morning of the 3rd. Numerous trees, power poles, and power lines were blown down,  resulting in fairly widespread power outages. The northern foothill counties appeared to the the hardest hit. In McDowell County, several homes and vehicles were damaged by falling trees. In Caldwell County, the roof of the County office building was damaged in Lenoir, and at least two homes were damaged by falling trees in the northern part of the county.

Third of the roof blown off a fire station east of Midland, near the county line.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wilmington ASOS measured an 89 mph wind gust.  A private hangar at the airport was damaged, and a man in Wilmington was injured.  In downtown Wilmington, a section of the roof of city hall was damaged, and an historic home, built in 1738, was moderately damaged when a chimney collapsed.  The Oceanic pier also measured a 69 mph wind gust.
A NWS Mesonet site measured an 89 mph wind gust.
A 72 mph measured gust was recorded at the Surf City bridge.

A 66 mph measured wind gust was recorded at the mesonet station just south of the Carolina Beach pier.  There was damage to three houses under construction on Bowfin Rd. One structure lost a roof. Palm trees and scaffolding in the area were blown over.
Lightning damage several buildings downtown. Numerous limbs were blown down.
Strong winds blew the roof off a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms produced large hail across northeastern portions of the area during the morning hours of March 28th.

High winds developed during the evening hours across the foothills, and far western piedmont, and continued through the overnight hours before subsiding during the pre-dawn hours. Numerous trees and power lines were blown down, resulting in quite a few power outages. At least one tree fell on a vehicle.

High winds developed during the evening hours across the foothills, and far western piedmont, and continued through the overnight hours before subsiding during the pre-dawn hours. Numerous trees and power lines were blown down, resulting in quite a few power outages. At least one tree fell on a vehicle.

Snow showers developed behind a cold front across the western mountains during the morning hours and continued through much of the day. By the time the snow tapered off to flurries during the evening, isolated accumulations as high as 6 inches were observed in the higher elevations along the Tennessee border. However, the valleys generally received less than 2 inches.

High winds developed across portions of the  mountains during the morning, mainly from the French Broad Valley north. Trees and power lines were blown down, resulting in some power outages. Several gusts were measured in the 60-70 mph range by home weather equipment. The most significant winds/damage occurred around Black Mountain.

Wet snow, heavy at times, developed in the higher elevations of the North Carolina mountains during the morning. By early evening, snowfall amounts ranged from trace amounts to 4 inches, mainly in areas above 3500 feet.  By early evening, some of the highest elevations reeached heavy snowfall criteria, mainly above 4000 feet. Light accumulations were reported in locations as low as 3000 feet. Meanwhile, the major valleys received mostly rain.

High winds developed across the mountains and foothills during the evening, and continued through the overnight hours before subsiding during the late morning of the 3rd. Numerous trees, power poles, and power lines were blown down,  resulting in fairly widespread power outages. The northern foothill counties appeared to the the hardest hit. In McDowell County, several homes and vehicles were damaged by falling trees. In Caldwell County, the roof of the County office building was damaged in Lenoir, and at least two homes were damaged by falling trees in the northern part of the county.

Snow showers redeveloped during the evening of the 28th, continuing off and on through the evening of the 1st. Additional snowfall accumulations of 1 to 2 inches were observed. The higher elevations along the Tennessee border were the main areas affected. However, some valley locations as far east as northern Buncombe and northern Jackson counties received light accumulations.

The higher elevations along the Tennessee border were the main areas affected. However, some valley locations as far east as northern Buncombe and northern Jackson counties received light accumulations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down in Lisbon.
Leland police reported several trees down.
A coop observer reported an estimated 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wet snow, mixed with rain and sleet at times, began falling across western North Carolina during the evening of the 27th, and continued into the morning hours of the 28th. Total snow accumulations ranged from a trace across the southwest mountain valleys and lower elevations of the foothills and piedmont south of I-40, to 2 inches in the higher elevations, and in piedmont and foothills locations north of I-40. By around sunrise on the 28th, most of the precipitation had changed to rain.

Total snow accumulations ranged from a trace across the southwest mountain valleys and lower elevations of the foothills and piedmont south of I-40, to 2 inches in the higher elevations, and in piedmont and foothills locations north of I-40. By around sunrise on the 28th, most of the precipitation had changed to rain.

Wet snow, mixed at times with rain and sleet developed during the early morning hours across western North Carolina, and persisted through late morning. By the time the snow ended, accumulations ranged from just a dusting in the southwest mountain valleys and southern piedmont areas, to as much as 3 inches in areas north of I-40. Slushy roads led to a few traffic accidents across the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree blown down onto Belvidere Road. The large tree fell and smashed a Ford Explorer. The force of the falling tree knocked the Ford Explorer into a light pole, killing the woman driver.

Power lines down.
Spotter reported wind gust of 65 mph.

Deep closed upper level low and associated surface low passed directly over northwest North Carolina on April 2nd.  The surface low deepened significantly as it exited and headed northeast. Unusualy strong low level winds developed in the deep, cyclonic flow in the storm's wake. The strongest wind gusts topped out in the 50 to 56 kt range for at least a one hour time frame. The winds caused widespread (mostly tree and powerline) damage over the favored high (cross-mountain flow) wind locations. Wet soil conditions likely aggravated the tree damage. There were also a few reports of structural damage (mainly roof and siding damage).The injury in Wilkes Co. occurred in Traphill.  A female was brusied and scratched when wind jerked open a door to her home as she turned the door knob to exit.  The door being blow open quickly by the wind caused her to fall into a vehicle parked outside.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado developed within the squall line as it moved over Charlotte metro. The tornado developed near the intersection of 36th and North Tryon streets, where the roof of a building was torn off. In the same general area, the roofs of 2 trailers were partially torn off. Intermittent tree damage occurred along most of the remaining 3-miles of the track, with some trees falling on vehicles. At the end of the track, the roof was damaged and some windows blown out when a large oak tree fell on Cochrane Middle School. The roof cover was torn off of a business and some large pine trees and limbs were blown down just south of the school.

Awnings torn off buildings in Salisbury. Power lines were downed in Franklin Township northwest of the city, and a roof was blown off a mobile home in the same area. A tree fell on a car southeast of the city, near the Stanly county line. The occupant was trapped for a while, but uninjured. 3000 customers lost power across the county.
Numerous trees down. At least 1 tree fell onto a garage.
Numerous trees and power lines blown down with about 100 power outages in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent snowfall resulted in heavy accumulations of wet snow in the high elevations of the North Carolina mountains, mainly in areas above 4000 feet. Accumulations were generally 4 to 6 inches, although some areas above 5000 feet received higher amounts, including 16 inches at Mount Mitchell, and 13 inches at Soco Gap. U.S. 19 and 441 were closed through the high elevations of the Balsams and Smokies. Several bicyclists were stranded in the snow in Jackson County, and had to be rescued.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado developed within a squall line as it passed just south of Gastonia. A funnel cloud was observed just west of highway 321, but the condensation funnel did not extend to the ground. Eyewitnesses ran inside their home, at which time the house reportedly shook. Shingles were torn off the roof of the home, and numerous large limbs were blown down.

A squall line developed in the lee of the mountains during the morning, and began producing damaging winds as it moved across the southern piedmont. Numerous trees, large limbs, and power lines were blown down in the county, with 2 trees falling on mobile homes. Some outbuildings were also damaged or destroyed. WFO Columbia relayed a report of a canopy on a gas station collapsing on some vehicles. A small airplane was flipped at AKH, where some sheet metal and a door was blown off a hangar. A tree also fell on and heavily damaged a mobile home in Mt Holly.
Tree fell on a car on Mount Holly Rd near NC 27. Several trees fell on homes along Beatties Ford Rd near LaSalle St. Some roofs were torn off buildings in this same area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey concluded a tornado touched down in the Mollie community.  An areal survey showed that the tornado tracked NNE and crossed several county roads, farm fields, and wooded areas.  The tornado produced three spots of F1 damage along its path with winds estimated between 73 and 112 mph.  Damage occurred to several homes, vehicles, outbuildings, and sheds, with many large trees down.
Nickel sized hail reported in Whitakers near US 301 and State Route 33.
The roofs were blown off of two mobile homes.  Several barns were heavily damaged.
Strong winds damaged a dozen pre-manufactured storage sheds.

Dime to penny sized hail at Burlington-Alamance Regional Airport.  48 mph wind gust also measured, and a 100-year-old maple tree fell on a home built in the early 1900s on Kirkpatrick Road in Burlington.
Power lines were blown down.
Businesses were flooded in town, along Highways 87, 54 and 49.
One and a half feet of water covered the intersection of Falls of the Neuse and Spring Forest Roads.  The flood waters floated an SUV.
Trees were blown down.
Nickel hail covering the ground.
Quarter sized hail was reported at the intersection of Sandy Forks and Falls of the Neuse Roads.  Numerous reports of dime sized hail elsewhere in Raleigh.
Trees were blown down on power lines on Highway 55 and Carpenter Road.
Trees were blown down along US Highway 64 Alternate between Rocky Mount and Kingsboro.
Five power poles were blown down with the wires still live, trapping thirteen people in eight different vehicles at Blue Ridge Road and Wade Avenue.

Mostly pea size hail, with some stones as large as nickels, accumulated to a depth of 2.5 inches in the Carters Ridge area south of Spruce Pine. Hail also accumulated to a depth of several inches in town. Several buildings received minor damage, mainly water damage due to ice dams developing on roofs and in gutters.
Dime to penny sized hail reported at exit 154 along Interstate 95.
Lightning struck a tree and caused it to fall across a road, temporarily blocking traffic.
Reported at Pisgah Forest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two swimmers at the Ramada Plaza in Kill Devil Hills were pulled away from the shore by a strong rip current during the mid afternoon. One man drowned.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to penny sized hail reported.
An Illinois man was drowned in a rip current on a Nags Head beach. The man was pulled from the water by lifeguards,  and pronounced dead later in the afternoon at an area hospital.

A waterspout moved onshore from the Atlantic side of Emerald Isle, and moved west for one mile before dissipating. A trained spotter reported shingles and tree limbs down on Lee Avenue. Tree damage was reported near the Emerald Isle Police Department.
Two metal industrial buildings sustained major damage and a barn was flattened. A home next to the barn also received minor roof damage. Several trees were snapped or uprooted.

Flooding began around Hendersonville, where portions of highway 25 were under water. Highway 64 was covered with water near Endeyville. Numerous other roads flooded across the county, some due to poor drainage, others due to flooding streams, which included Mud Creek and Clear Creek. At least 16 roads were closed across the county during the event. By around sunrise, flooding was quite severe, as 6 motorists required rescue from submerged vehicles. A lake overflowed at Camp Judea near Edneyville, damaging several vehicles and cabins, and necessitating evacuation of 175 campers.
Big Pine Creek flooded several roads and trapped some residents in their homes. Several private bridges and culverts were washed out.

Several inches of water covered the intersection of Falls of the Neuse and Spring Forest Roads.  Flood waters floated another vehicle, which blocked the intersection.  1.3 inches of rain fell in 20 minutes at Edwards Mill Road and Laurel Drive.
Trees were blown down.
Secondary roads and bridges were under water.
Trees were blown down.

About six inches of water covered the floors of businesses on Franklin Street after water backed out of a storm drainage system, covering the street, flooding buildings and the parking lot for the Police Department.  Cross Creek overflowed its banks and left mud on streets, sidewalks, and parking lots.  Twenty streets were closed due to flooding.
Trees down along highway 64.
Wind blew down 3 trees at scattered locations across the county.
Trees down along highway 64. Trees were also reported down around Sugarloaf Mountain.
Trees down along highway 64.
Trees down. Also, power was out in town for a while in the morning, but it is unclear whether lightning or wind was the culprit.
Severe urban flooding developed, with 2 cars stranded in water on Sugar Creek Rd. There was also water over North Tryon St.
Report of 3 trees blown down in the Luck area.
Trees and power lines down.
Hail reported hail on Interstate 40 between Hickory and Conover.
Trees blown down.
County reports a tree blown onto a house.
A few trees down in the city. One fell on a house, causing damage.
Penny hail at intersection of Jennings and Snow Creek Roads 10 miles north of Statesville. Another spotter reported nickel hail 4 to 6 miles north of Statesville.

Two spotters reported dime to penny hail in south Mecklenburg County until 8:15.
Hail reported at Lake James.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonable and strong Nor'easter buffeted the North Carolina coast on the 6th with damaging wind gusts, torrential rain, high surf, and coastal flooding. Winds were sustained as high as 45 to 55 mph with wind gusts to 80 mph across coastal counties of Eastern North Carolina. Water levels rose four to six feet above normal along Pamlico Sound, and the lower reaches of the Neuse River. Storm total rainfall amounts ranged from 4 to 7 inches.During the peak of storm, the Diamond buoy reported 20 foot waves. Moderate beach erosion was reported along the Outer Banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported sound-side flooding with one foot of water on Highway 12 near Frisco.
Mt. Carmel Road closed due to high water from creek overflowing its banks.
Marble to penny sized hail along US Highway 29 between Thomasville and Lexington.
Penny sized hail reported on US Highway 52.
Large tree limbs were blown down, covering US Highway 158.
Penny sized hail reported at the Durham Correctional Center.
Trees were blown down and an outbuilding was damaged on Westview Drive.
Three to four trees were blown down on Davis Road.

Spring Creek left its banks and threatened 3 mobile homes in the Luck area.
Water up to the windows of a car.

Thunderstorm winds during the evening of 6th downed trees in Pine Hill in Stokes county. A thunderstorms during the evening of the 6th produced penny sized hail 7 miles east of Ayersville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding developed on the Cullasaja River and some of its tributaries between the communities of Cullasaja and Highlands, closing several roads and forcing the evacuation of a campground. The rising river washed out a new sewer line near Wells Grove Rd. Flooded tributaries included Nickajack Creek and Turtle Pond Creek, which forced evacuation of at least 1 residence. Closed roads included Turtle Pond Creek and River Roads.  A 2-day rainfall total of 7.9 inches was reported at Scaly Mountain.
Many roads flooded in downtown Kinston and closed to traffic.
Widespread flooding reported across the county with many roads flooded and closed.
Large trees down in Deerfield off of Gumbranch Road.
Large trees down on Highway 41 West.
Large tree down

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced damaging winds along Interstate 77 west and northwest of Pine Ridge.  One location on the interstate highway had trees across it, and at a truck weigh station, trees came down on top of vehicles.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports of large tree limbs down on power lines.
Waterspout moved onshore then quickly dissipated. Some outdoor furniture at one residence was damaged.
Several large trees blown down.

Flooding developed first along the Davidson River in Pisgah National Forest, forcing a road closure. A section of highway 64 was closed near Sapphire. Other road closures included Richland Creek Rd in Lake Toxaway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds during the evening of 6th downed trees in Pine Hill in Stokes county. A thunderstorms during the evening of the 6th produced penny sized hail 7 miles east of Ayersville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Richland Creek flooded Riverbend Rd.
A creek overflowed its banks, flooding part of Franklin Bvd.
Flooding of Poplar Tent and Odell Schools roads west and southwest of Kannapolis.
A small creek overflowed its banks near Stateville Ave, with water surrounding a home. At least one road was covered with water.
Webb Road flooded in the vicinity of Glover Rd and Mt Hope Church Rd. Report of 10 to 12 inches of water covering Old Concord Rd in this same area.
Lightning sparked several structural fires in the county.
A mudslide was reported on Holbert Cove Rd and a culvert was washed out on Skyuka Mountain Rd.
A womans face was burned when the phone she was talking on exploded during a power surge caused by lightning.

After a night of moderate to heavy rain, flooding developed along some creeks in the northern part of the county during the morning. The first stream that flooded was Goose Creek, which flooded near the intersection of highways 601 and 218. Flooding from the South Fork of Crooked Creek later threatened a home on station road. Several roads were closed because of high water, including Goldmine, Mill Grove, and Matthew-Weddington roads.

Flooding first began along several creeks in the Midland area, including Clear, Muddy, and Little Meadow, as well as some small tributaries of the Rocky River. Later in the morning, the Rocky River flooded in the northwest part of the county, near Poplar Tent Rd. Several roads were closed due to high water, including Hopewell Church Rd, where several homes were surrounded by high water.
Flooding of creeks with water beginning to threaten trailer parks.
Creeks came out of their banks around Marshall. Water covered portions of highway 213 at the junction of highways 25 and 70. Rollins Rd was also covered with water.
Lightning sparked a fire out an outbuilding on Cowee Ridge. The structure was destroyed.
Lightning ignited a house fire near the Laurel Park Shopping center. The fire destroyed much of the roof.
Tree trees snapped off by lightning.
Lightning sparked a house fire, causing extensive damage to the structure and its contents.
A person was struck by lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on Tusquittee Road and Downings Creek Road.
Damaging thunderstorm winds downed trees in the area around Courtney.

The remnants of Tropical Storm Cindy spawned some tornadoes.  The tornadoes in Yadkin County NC caused numerous trees, some large oaks, to be felled or snapped off.  Structural damage included roofs of some buildings being damaged or completely blown off and an outbuilding being blown off its foundation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Columbus emergency manager reported heavy rain ponding affected twelve to fifteen homes and stores in a five block area of downtown Chadbourne.
Intersection of College Rd. and 17th St. under a foot of water with traffic flow disrupted.

In Stokes County the damage consisted of some trees down and a shed blown over.  In Stokes County there were numerous trees down between Mayodan and Stoneville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees down countywide.  Reported by the Sheriff's Office.
Golfball-size hail reported in Murphy, by the public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down across West Vineyard Road.
In Stokes County the damage consisted of some trees down and a shed blown over.  In Stokes County there were numerous trees down between Mayodan and Stoneville.

Close to 7 inches of rain fell on Beech Mountain in southwest Watauga Co in the late afternoon and early evening of the 19th.  The resulting flash flooding resulted in roads closed with water and debris on them, as well as, numerous driveways washed out.  Some of the roadways had minor damaged caused to them.

A severe thunderstorm produced damaging winds along Interstate 77 west and northwest of Pine Ridge.  One location on the interstate highway had trees across it, and at a truck weigh station, trees came down on top of vehicles.

Flash flooding occurred along portions of Tom's Creek near the town of Westfield and caused a road washout and some property damage.  Radar estimated 3 inches of rain in less than an hour in the flash flood region.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down on highway 176 between Tryon and Saluda.
Trees also down in Spindale around the same time.
Trees, large limbs, and an awning blown down at Lake James campground at Benfield Landing.
Trees and a large limbs down on Warren Bridge Rd near the Wilkes County line.

A severe thunderstorm produced extremely heavy rainfall across northern portions of Cabarrus County. A spotter reported several roads under as much as a foot of water, including portions of Sisk-Carter Rd and Shuping Mill Rd.

A tributary of Little Sugar Creek flooded Bradbury and Montford drives in south Charlotte. Two vehicles were trapped in flood water, and their drivers had to be rescued. Several homes were threatened for a while, and at least 1 home had water up to the top of the front porch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large tree down due to damaging thunderstorm winds.
Severe thunderstorms, some with bowing line segments, resulted in trees and some power lines down.
Severe thunderstorms, some with bowing line segments, resulted in trees and some power lines down.
Severe thunderstorms, some with bowing line segments, resulted in trees and some power lines down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning hit a gas line on Flat Mountain Rd, which caused a nereby house to catch fire.

Severe urban flooding in Lenoir, with cars stalled out on highway 18, Creekway Dr,  and  Morganton Bvd. Several small creeks also flooded in the vicinity of town. Water entered the basements of several homes and businesses, including the Emergency Services building.
For the second consecutive evening, severe urban flooding developed in Lenoir and small streams flooded around town. Several roads were closed throughout the city.

A very small, but persistent rain shower produced 5 inches of rain in 2 hours, resulting in flash flooding in a small area in the Wesser community. Wesser Creek flooded near its confluence with the Nantahala River, covering several roads with water. Water entered several homes in this area as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms, some with bowing line segments, resulted in trees and some power lines down.
Severe thunderstorms, some with bowing line segments, resulted in trees and some power lines down.
Severe thunderstorms, some with bowing line segments, resulted in trees and some power lines down.

Spotter in the Beaucatcher Mountain area reported mostly pea size hail, with a few stones up to nickel size.
Several trees blown down.
Limbs down.
Numerous trees down across the county.
A couple trees down in the Carowinds area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm during the afternoon of the 7th produced hail up to quarter sized in Stokes county.
A severe thunderstorm during the afternoon of the 7th produced hail up to quarter sized in Stokes county.
Thunderstorm winds downed trees in the town of Danbury on the evening of the 21st.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent, widespread heavy rain showers associated with moisture from tropical storm Arlene resulted in flooding across the southern mountains. County reported the Tuckaseegee was out of its banks in several locations, with several roads closed, including Shook Cove Rd, Dillard Rd, and North & South River Rd. As water was released from various dams along the river, the flooding persisted well after heavy rain ended across the area.
Numerous trees blown down across the entire county, with most trees down in Pleasant Grove.
Trees down near Forest City, some on roads and houses, and power lines down on highway 221 south of Rutherfordton.
Trees and power lines were blown down near NC 731.
Several trees down around Fontana Lake and highway 28.
Street flooding in Highlands, at least part of the flooding was due to Mill Creek overflowing its banks.
Several trees and power lines blown down in the Granite Quarry area. Part of a large tree snapped and another tree blown over in Salisbury.
Several trees blown down. Report of funnel in the same area.
Hail up to the size of nickels piled up along the side of the road in the Fines Creek area.
3 inch diamter limbs down.
A couple trees down in the Lake Norman area.
Hail of unknown size at the intersection of I-85 and highway 273 and along highway 74 at the Catawba River in the Belmont area. Pea to nickel size hail in Mt Holly.
Nickel to quarter sized hail at the intersection of highways 182 and 27.

Flooding began across the county with severe urban and poor drainage flooding, mainly in areas from Monroe to Matthews. Several major roads were flooded through Monroe, including highway 74 and Rocky River Rd. By early afternoon, flooding had become quite serious across the county, as a few overflowing creeks and streams contributed to the problem. Flooded creeks included Stump Lick Branch, Bearskin Creek, and Stewarts Creek. In all, 30 to 35 roads were closed across the county due to high water.
Lightning ignited fires at 2 homes.

Several mudslides around the city accompanied a flash flood. One mudslide took down a utility pole and destroyed part of a parking lot. Another collapsed a retaining wall, which in turn damaged a sewer line.
A small pond overflowed near Valdese, flooding part of highway 10. This typically happens during heavy rain.

Mainly pea size hail fell for about 30 minutes and accumulated to a depth of 3 incehs in the Penrose area. The hail was still on the ground the next day. County reported damage to apple crops. There was a rumor of golfball size hail in this area as well, but this could not be confirmed.
Quite a few trees, power lines, and power poles down, with at least 2 trees on houses.
Numerous roads flooded in the northern part of the county.
Charley Creek Rd was flooded near its intersection with highway 281, possibly due to flooding along Sols Creek.
Golf Course Rd was closed due to flooding.
Lightning ignited a house fire on Chickadee Trail, causing significant damage.
Lightning was responsible for at least 6 house fires across the city.
Several private driveways were washed out on Nix Creek Rd and a 25 to 30 feet diameter sink hole opened on Sugar Hill Rd.
Lightning was suspected to have ignited a fire at a mobile home, which caused extensive damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 20th produced hail up to penny size and downed trees in the Mount Airy area.
Thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 20th produced hail up to penny size and downed trees in the Mount Airy area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck near a lifeguard on Wrightsville Beach.  The lifeguard suffered muscle spasms and was treated and released at the local hospital.
Flooding was reported on 17th St. and Oleander, as well as Dawson St.

Straight-line winds flattened a significant portion of a bean field in the Leggett area near the Sandy Acres Mobile Home Park.  In the mobile home park, four mobile homes were destroyed and six others were damaged.  Two of the destroyed mobile homes were flipped over, their anchors and tie down straps having been snapped or pulled completely out of the ground.  The area of damage originated near the interesection of West Logsboro and Battleboro Roads just south of Highway 97, and extended east for about four miles to Highway 33.  Snapped trees resulted in damage to roofs along West Logsboro Road and Highway 33 from East Logsboro Road to Fishing Creek Road.  At its widest point the damage area was as much as two miles wide.

Approximately 30 trees were blown down in Vass.  A vehicle and a business were both damaged when trees fell on them.  Trees were reported down in Southern Pines as well, where a tree damaged a home when it fell.
Heavy rains caused flooding on Patetown Road, Big Daddy's Road, and Indian Springs Road, all in different parts of the county.  Other secondary roads had high water reported as well.
Penny size hail reportd at Nash and Airport Boulevard.  The hail lasted for several minutes.
A house was struck by lightning in Holly Grove.  Damage unknown.

Flooding was first reported between Oak Hill and Lake James, where roads had become impassable due to high water. Steels Creek overflowed its banks, flooding Linville Rd and surrounding a campground on highway 181, from which several people required rescue. Other flooded roads included Fish Hatchery Rd, Frank Whisnant Rd, Bost Rd, and Causby Rd. Muddy Creek and Reedy Creek also flooded near the McDowell line.
A portion of highway 126 was closed due to stream flooding.
Flooding developed on Wilson Creek, with water over a bridge on Brown Mountain Rd  and another bridge on Playmore Beach Rd. Flooding also developed on the Johns River.
Trees down. One foot diameter limbs broken off other trees.
Large limbs down on power lines in N. Davidson County.
Penny size hail on Highway 62 just S of Alamance.
Trees down near Parktown.
Flooding developed along Allen Creek, with several streets covered in water, including Browning Bridge Rd, which was closed. Flood water surrounded several homes.

Little Savannah Creek came out of its banks, flooding Blake Branch Rd. Later in the evening, the Tuckasegee River came out of its banks in this same area and flooded low lying areas, including the area around a trailer park, from which some residents required evacuation.
Trees down in the Collinsville Rd area.
Golfball size hail in the Patterson Springs area.
Trees down.
A tree and power line down.
Quite a few trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the remnants of hurricane Katrina moved across middle and west Tennessee, high winds developed over the mountains of North Carolina. Numerous trees and power lines were blown down, with damage being most concentrated in the southwest mountains, and in Avery County of the northern mountains. At least 2 trees fell on and damaged structures.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: After an extended period of moderate to heavy rainfall in association with the remnants of hurricane Katrina, flooding of small streams and creeks developed across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 20th produced hail up to penny size in Francisco.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although heavy rain abated somewhat during the early morning hours, flooding continued past sunrise. A total of 25 roads were closed during the event.
Flooding continued well after the end of the heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the remnants of hurricane Katrina moved across middle and west Tennessee, high winds developed over the mountains of North Carolina. Numerous trees and power lines were blown down, with damage being most concentrated in the southwest mountains, and in Avery County of the northern mountains. At least 2 trees fell on and damaged structures.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down on highway 276 south of Brevard.
A tree 12 inches in diameter was blown down onto a house, causing roof damage.
Wind gust of 51 knots (59 mph) measured at ECG.
Trees down.
Brief F0 touchdown in a field near the Coast Guard Base.
Weather photographer reported rotation near tree top level extending up to cloud base in forested area.

Flash-flood producing rainfall across the county was the cause of a small landslide resulting in a layer of rocks and debris covering Ridgecrest Road near Jacob and Austin Roads, between the towns of Locust and Oakboro.  A couple of other locations across the county reported thick mud and silt across the road, some of them the result of runoff from construction sites.
A weak tornado briefly touched down on Morton-Pulliam Road near City Lake, knocking over several trees.

There were numerous reports of hail around the Pittsboro area.  Quarter size hail was reported at the intersection of Highways 902 and 87.  Several reports of dime to penny size hail were received as well.
Numerous trees and powerlines were blown down along U.S. 15-501 near the Orange County line.
Trees were blown down in Holly Grove.
The tops of trees blown out on Sloans Mill Rd.
Trees were blown down.
Several creeks overflowed their banks in locations from Skyland to Asheville Regional Airport, flooding roads, including Mills Gap and Hendersonville Rds, and some buildings.
A couple trees down.
Several trees were blown down and a tobacco crop was severely damaged in downburst winds.
A smoldering tree which had been struck by lightning a few hours earlier fell, killing a firefighter.

Lightning struck a home in Sanford as storms passed just north of town. The lightning strike initiated a fire in the crawl space which consumed the home. No one was in the house at the time of the fire.
Quarter size hail was reported at the corner of Aversboro and Timber Drives.
Trees were blown down on Old Iron Works Road.
Penny size hail was reported as well as numerous large limbs blown down at Wayne Memorial Drive and Soften Road.
Penny size hail was reported at Daniels Street and Kay Dee Drive.
Severe urban flooding, as well as flooding of small creeks around Lincolnton. Water entered some homes.
A portion of highway 18 was closed south of Fallston. Also, a portion of highway 226 was closed due to flooding of a creek.
Cox Creek was reported to be out of its banks just south of Little Switzerland.
Grassy Creek was reported to be out out of its banks in the community of Grassy Creek.
Severe flooding along Little Creek, with water in several homes and a few  roads closed.

Severe urban flooding developed briefly in Mooresville. Flooded streets included portions of Main and Broad, as well as Stateville Ave. Several roads were closed due to high water. Also, a bridge on Linwood Rd was flooded.

County reported significant flash flooding along Wilsons Creek in the Edgemont and Mortimer areas, where the creek was as much as 5 feet out of its banks. Edgemont Church Rd was underwater, flooding at least one home. Brown Mtn Rd was also flooded. 51 people were evacuated in the Mortimer area, while swift water rescues were required in some instances. Several bridges over the creek were covered with water. Flooding also developed downstream in the Brown Mountain Beach area.
Trees and power lines down.
Trees down near the Rutherford County line.
Trees down on power lines on South Sterling St.
Several trees down in town.

Severe urban flooding in Hickory and Conover, with a bridge washed out in Conover. Also, a sinkhole that developed in 2002 in a resteraunt parking lot in Hickory during a significant flash flood event reopened, while a second sinkhole developed in another resteraunt parking lot.
Newspaper reported a bridge on Johns River Rd was covered with water.

Flooding from creeks and streams in the Patterson area, including the Yadkin River, which forced closure of highway 268. Flooding also developed in the Collettsville area where several bridges were covered with water due to flooding along Wilson Creek and Johns River. Closed roads included Old Johns River Rd, Brown Mtn Beach Rd.
Although heavy rain ended across the area during the early afternoon, high water conditions continued for several hours, with quite a few roads closed.

Trees and power lines blown down along a half to three quarter mile swath off highway 74 west of Shelby. In addition, some shingles were removed from a house and a porch was shifted from its foundation.
Seven trees blown down.

Five mile long, one quarter mile wide damage swath from near the highway 601 and 801 intersection to just west of Farmington. The damage path was intermittent, with mainly trees, large limbs, and power lines blown down. A house near the end of the damage path received some minor roof damage.
Golfball size hail was reported on South Plank Road.
Nickel size hail was reported on Wildlife Road, in the Lemon Springs Wildlife Refuge.
Numerous trees were blown down on US Highway 64 East and Old Milburnie Road near the Neuse River.
A trained spotter measured a 60 mph gust at Ten-Ten and Holland Church Roads as the gust front passed.  A gust of 57 mph was measured two minutes later.
Trees were blown down along SR 742 near Cason Oil Field.
Numerous trees and power lines were blown down.
Severe urban flooding developed in Rosman, with several streets closed, including Main Street. Water entered several buildings.
A few trees down.
Although the heavy rain abated during the early morning hours, bringing an end to flash flooding, roads remained closed and/or covered with water through much of the pre-dawn hours.
Heavy rain falling over Rowan County produced flooding along Irish Buffalo Creek near Kannapolis. This required evacuation of around 70 people from a nursing home and a mobile home park.
F0 (Rope) tornado reported near Highway 17.

Lightning struck the beach at Bald Head Island, injuring nine people.  Seven were treated at the local hospital for tingling feelings.  One boy was in cardiac arrest when rescue units arrived and was revived at the scene.  Another boy was knocked unconcious and was also revived at the scene.  All the victims were out of the water and on the sand at the time of the strike.
Water covered the road at the intersection of College Road and South 17th Street to a depth of one foot.  Several nearby lawns had standing water to a depth of eight inches.
Reports of numerous roadways flooded from Whiteville to Cerro Gordo.
Golfball size hail was reported in Research Triangle Park.

Straight-line winds damaged businesses in the area of Skibo and Morgantown Roads.  Wind damage consisted of blown out windows, broken store front facades, and blown down signs.  Numerous trees were blown down as well, and all the damage was laid out in a fan pattern.  The area of damage was about a half mile wide and a half mile long.
Although the heavy rain abated during the early morning hours, bringing an end to flash flooding, roads remained closed and/or covered with water through much of the pre-dawn hours.
Flooding along Fountain Creek, with water covering Hebron Rd.

Severe urban flooding in the city, with at least 3 people trapped in flooded vehicles on Main and Innes Streets. Several small creeks flooded around town (including tributaries of Grants Creek and the creek itself in Spencer), with water entering the basement of at least one home. Severely flooded roads included 18th St, Mahaley Ave, and roads around Knox Middle School and Overton School.
Penny to nickel size hail along with large tree limbs down.
Penny size hail reported near Highway 24.
Penny size hail reported south of Jacksonville from near Camp Lejeune to near Verona.
Large tree limbs blown down.
Public reported a waterspout moved onshore near Emerald Isle, then quickly dissipated.

A tornado touched down in Swann near the Harnett-Lee County Line. Several trees were blown down and twisted, and a few shingles were blown off of houses.  The tornado lifted and touched down intermittently as it traveled northeast, causing minor tree damage.  The tornado lifted after touching down a final time in Cokesbury.

A weak tornado touched down near Lewisville, blowing several trees onto homes there and in Pfafftown as well.  The tornado touched down repeatedly as it traveled northeast, finally lifting at Rural Hall.  Mostly tree damage was reported along the path, at Highway 52 and Westinghouse Road, Boiling Springs Road, Ridge Road, and along NC Highway 67.

Heavy rainfall flooded a golf course with several inches of water, and the back yards of several homes had high water as well.  Several ponds and small lakes overflowed and threatened homes.  A spotter reported 6.79 inches of rainfall in just over five hours.
Trees were blown down.
Several trees were blown down in Sanford.  One tree fell on a house at Hawkins Road and Fairway Drive, severely damaging it.
Trees were blown down on SR 1401 between Robbins and the county line.
Trees were blown down.
Trees were blown down on Old Smithfield Road.
Several trees down near Murphy
Two trees down near of Hayesville.
A few trees and powerlines down in western half of county.  Reported by Blue Ridge Mountain Power Co.

Severe urban flooding, with around a foot of water on several roads off highway 152 in the Landis and China Grove areas, including Wilkinson Rd. Grants Creek flooded on the north side of Kannapolis, with Pine St underwater.
Several large limbs were blown down on Highway 54 south of Durham.  Residents had to use chainsaws to cut the limbs in order to get out of their driveways.
Trees were blown down.
Heavy rains flooded several streets in Roanoke Rapids.  Three feet of water covered Becker Drive at Interstate 95, and two feet of water covered the intersection of Highway 158 and Premier Boulevard.
Dime to penny size hail was reported near the intersection of State Route 581 and Harrell Store Road.
Golfball size hail was reported along with a downed tree on Route 561 and Tanner Road.
Several large trees were blown down across southern Hoke County.  A few trees fell on homes, causing minor property damage.
Quarter size hail was reported along Highway 87 and Buffalo Lake Road.
The roof of a mobile home was damaged near the intersection of US 401 Bypass and State Route 24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the remnants of hurricane Katrina moved across middle and west Tennessee, high winds developed over the mountains of North Carolina. Numerous trees and power lines were blown down, with damage being most concentrated in the southwest mountains, and in Avery County of the northern mountains. At least 2 trees fell on and damaged structures.

As the remnants of hurricane Katrina moved across middle and west Tennessee, high winds developed over the mountains of North Carolina. Numerous trees and power lines were blown down, with damage being most concentrated in the southwest mountains, and in Avery County of the northern mountains. At least 2 trees fell on and damaged structures.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.
Numerous trees were blown down along Route 49 just southwest of Asheboro.
Trees were blown down in Clayton, and minor damage was sustained to a pickup truck.
Middle Creek flooded near the community of Otto, with two homes surrounded by water. Flooding developed later in the evening in low spots along the Little Tennessee River near Franklin.
Long Branch creek overflowed and flooded Neds Chapel Rd.
Two roads flooded near the Madison County line. A private bridge was also washed out near Leicester
Two roads closed in Morganton, at least one of which was flooded by an overflowing creek.
Flat Creek Valley Rd was barricaded due to flooding.
A large tree limb snapped off, falling on and injuring a woman. Another part of the same tree fell on a house, trapping the people inside.
Part of a hillside gave way, damaging the Broad River VFDs building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of weather systems including the remnants of Tropical Storm Tammy and low pressure associated with an approaching cold front linked up to cause flooding rains across the area. During a three day period from October 6th through the 8th portions of eastern North Carolina received up to a foot of rainfall. Six to eight inch rainfall totals were common across much of the area. This resulted in flash flooding and widespread flooding across Beaufort, Carteret, Craven, Duplin, Lenoir, Martin, Onslow, Pamlico, and Pitt counties. Many roads across the area were closed due to flooding, and property damage was reported in several counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State of emergency declared for the city of Washington. Flood damage reported in the downtown area from 3rd to 15th streets.

The remnants of Tropical Storm Tammy, in conjunction with a weak upper level disturbance in the Caribbean set up an unusually long and sustained fetch of tropical moisture over the area.  The heaviest rain occurred near the coast, where seven to nine inches of rain fell on average over a three day period.  Even more rain fell over eastern Brunswick and New Hanover county, where over a foot of rain fell.  Over 50 roads in Brunswick county alone were either partially or completely impassable.  In New Hanover county, the Cape Fear Museum's basement was completely flooded.  Over 46,000 rare artifacts were stored in this area.  Many residencial areas had roads replaced by small lakes, with numerous homes and some businesses sustaining minor to moderate flood damage.  In Robeson county, State Highway 130 was barricaded due to high water.  Pender county also saw flooding, with portions of Highway 117 under water.  Much of the monetary damages were due to debris cleanup, road repair, and water damage to homes and businesses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting to 60 mph brought trees down in the Boone North Carolina area, as well as across Ashe County.

Category one Hurricane Ophelia, with maximum sustained winds of 85 mph, approached the North Carolina coast on the 13th. The hurricane remained offshore brushing the southern coastal counties of Onslow and Carteret on the 14th and 15th. Highest winds and damages occurred across this area where winds gusted to near 100 mph, and storm surges of up to 6 feet resulted in structural damages totaling near 35 million dollars. The highest surge was reported along the lower reaches of the Neuse River in southern Craven County where water levels rose to 8 feet, during the night of the 14th. Ophelia brushed by Outer Banks Hyde and Dare counties on the 16th with hurricane force wind gusts, primarily across coastal Hyde county where minor flooding and structural damage occurred. Minor wind damage occurred across the inland counties of Duplin, Jones, Lenoir, and Craven where tropical storm force wind gusts blew shingles off roofs, and downed trees and power lines. The combination of surge from Pamlico Sound and heavy storm total rainfall, from 4 to 9 inches, resulted in the flooding of streams, roads, and lower elevations in Beaufort, Carteret, Craven, Jones, Onslow, and Pamlico counties.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many roads flooded in and around Greenville and Grimesland.
Trees were blown down on Interstate 85 near mile marker 170.  Trees were also blown down north of the interstate on Willet Road.
Trees blown down along Route 1628
Southern part of Greenville flooded with three feet of water covering roadways.
Water up to 2 feet deep reported on roadways from Ayden to Winterville.
Large Tree limbs blown out of trees in addition to large hail.
Penny to quarter size hail reported in the Greenville area.

Severe urban flooding developed in Lincolnton, with a number of streets and roads closed, including highway 27. Ham report of water up to waist-deep on East Main St, with several cars stranded. Water entered some homes, with evacuations of mobile homes becoming necessary in low lying areas. Ham radio reported swift water running through a mobile home park in Boger City.
Beach Springs Road closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the remnants of hurricane Katrina moved across middle and west Tennessee, high winds developed over the mountains of North Carolina. Numerous trees and power lines were blown down, with damage being most concentrated in the southwest mountains, and in Avery County of the northern mountains. At least 2 trees fell on and damaged structures.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quite a few large trees blown down, especially on Cherry Mountain Rd. One tree fell on a home, causing minor damage. Another tree fell on a vehicle and other trees fell on power lines.
Trees and power lines down.

The body of a migrant worker was found on Walker Farm in Person County. The worker complained of sickness and left the farm around 1130am. The worker was later found dead only a quarter mile away in a creek bed. The heat index by 11am was 103 degrees.

Five inches of rain fell in and around Goldsboro flooding several roads including major roads such as Wayne Memorial Drive and Stoney Creek. Numerous secondary roads were flooded and closed in low lying areas.
Trees down on Hibriten Rd. Tree also across Norwood St, and a tree on a house on Winchester St.
Several trees blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the remnants of hurricane Katrina moved across middle and west Tennessee, high winds developed over the mountains of North Carolina. Numerous trees and power lines were blown down, with damage being most concentrated in the southwest mountains, and in Avery County of the northern mountains. At least 2 trees fell on and damaged structures.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited a fire at a house, causing significant damage to the home.
Lightning ignited a fire at a house, completely destroying the structure.
Highway dept employee reported several trees down on her property, with quite a few trees down on a neighboring property. Also, PD reported hail, almost to dime size, in Elk Park around this time.

Gusty winds developed over the North Carolina mountains and foothills as the remnants of hurricance Katrina passed west of the region. A few trees and power lines were blown down here and there, with at least one tree on a house in Hickory.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the remnants of hurricane Katrina moved across middle and west Tennessee, high winds developed over the mountains of North Carolina. Numerous trees and power lines were blown down, with damage being most concentrated in the southwest mountains, and in Avery County of the northern mountains. At least 2 trees fell on and damaged structures.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and large limbs down on S Forest Ln.
30 to 40 trees down in the area around Old Murphy Rd near Standing Indian Campground.
Report of 1.75 inches of rain in 20 minutes, with Bull Creek beginning to come out of its banks.
Savannah Creek came out of its banks in spots, but flooding was confined to fields.
Rainfall of around 5 inches caused 2 debris-flows in the area around Jackson County airport, taking down several trees and flowing over the driveway of a residence.
Street flooding developed in a construction area, with some cars stalled and rescue of 2 motorists required.

Urban flooding developed in south Charlotte, when 3 inches of rain fell in about an hour and a half. Several vehicles stalled at a nursing home when the parking lot flooded. Based on newspaper reports, the flooding appears to have been mainly the result of clogged drains.
Tornado touched down near exit 20 off highway 321 producing mainly F0 damage, with some intermittent F1. A number of trees were snapped off and there was damage to a cornfield.
Trees down.
Quite a few trees and power lines blown down.
Several trees and power lines blown down.
Five large trees blown down on 13th St, with some windows broken out. One tree fell on a vehicle. At least one other tree and several large limbs were blown down elsewhere in town.
Several trees and power lines blown down. At least 1 tree fell on a vehicle.

Several roads across the county were closed due to flooding, mainly in the Columbus and Mill Spring areas.  Portions of highway 9, Big Level Rd, Landrum Rd, Fox Mountain Rd, and Hunting Country Rd were closed.

Numerous roads closed due to flooding, mainly in the Rutherfordton, Spindale, Forest City and Bostic areas, including portions of highways 321 and 221. Mountain Creek flooded a portion of NC 108. At least 1 family in Rutherford required rescue when water surrounded their home. At least 3 homes and 4 businesses were flooded on South Main in Rutherfordton. Several bridges were washed out, including one on Hines Rd in the far southeast part of the county, and Wells Rd and Terry Rd in Bostic.  A cement bridge was washed out on Old Henrietta Rd. Thompson Rd was washed out in the southwest part of the county.  Maple Creek Rd in Rutherfordton was underwater in 4 locations.
Penny size hail was reported at the intersection of Shipyard Boulevard and Carolina Beach Rd.

Several road closures due to high water, mainly in the Hendersonville area, including Blackwell Dr, Fruitland Rd, South Main, Airport Rd, and at least 4 others. A portion of highway 25 was closed north of Hendersonville. Jeffress Rd was also flooded in the Mills River area.
Four families evacuated from an apartment complex due to flooding. One resident reported 6-8 inches of water entered his apartment.
Lightning ignited a house fire, which caused significant damage to the structure.
A tree fell through a mobile home on Whitmire Rd.
A mudslide off of highway 64 between Rosman and Lake Toxaway moved a mobile home from its foundation, rendering it uninhabitable.
Lightning struck a high-voltage power line, knocking out power to 1850 residents.
Lightning knocked out power to about 6000 customers in the Matthews area.
Motorists stranded on several flooded roads including Elizabeth Street, Forest Park Road, and Route 17 near the Highway Patrol Station.
Numerous roads closed particularly in Camden.
Lightning struck a home, igniting a fire which destroyed the structure.
Lightning struck a home, igniting a fire which completely destroyed the structure and its contents.
Several structural fires caused by lightning.
Lightning struck a house, which ignited a fire that destroyed the porch and damaged the main part of the house.
Trees down.
Power lines down.
Trees down on Hanks Chapel Road.
A couple trees down on the west side of town.
2 trees down.
Several trees down near Fairview.
Two power poles blown down in town, one of which fell on a business. Several large trees also fell in town, one of which damaged a house. About half the city residents lost power during the storm.
A couple trees down.
Several trees down.
Trees down.
Several trees down in Hickory and surrounding areas. Also, some power lines were blown down, knocking out power to about 4000 people.

Three large trees were blown down in a yard, taking down a power transformer with them.  Much of the city's power was out for most of the evening.  Several other trees and power lines were blown down throughout the town.
A few trees were blown down.
A few secondary roads were flooded and had to be closed by the DOT.
Lightning struck three houses with minor roof damage.  A riding lawn mower was also damaged.
Lightning struck two homes in Long Beach.  Minor damage to the interior of the homes was reported.
Law enforcement reported numerous flooded roadways across Wilmington.
Racine Drive was closed due to flooding.  Both Pilgrim Circle and Castle Hayne Road were flooded.  Numerous other roads in the county were reported flooded.

Flash floods waters caused roads to be closed throughout the county after small cars tried to cross the roads but could not get through.  Highway 73 and Millingport Road just outside of Albemarle, Big Lick Road and Oak Ridge Road near Oakboro, and Mission Church Road near Locust were the areas hardest hit.
Trees were blown down at the intersection of Hazard and Barbie's Grove Roads, and several trees were blown down along St. Martin Road.
Trees were blown down at the intersection of Cottonville and Old Cotton Roads.

An estimated four to six inches of rain fell in less than two hours over Mount Olive.  Most streets in town were covered with water.  The Piggy Wiggly supermarket had to be closed so that people could retrieve their vehicles from the flooded parking lot.  A couple of people were stranded and water rose to the bottom of the driver's side window on some cars.  Water made it into a few businesses, and a sinkhole opened up on one road.  A driveway on Crest Drive was washed away as well.
A tornado blew down trees from Oak Ridge to Stokesdale.
Lightning started a fire in a house on Joel Lane.
A migrant worker collapsed and died from heat stroke on Harnett County near Erwin. The man collapsed on a tobacco farm and was transported to UNC Hospital where he later died.
Trees were blown down.
Several trees were blown down at Jordan Lake just north of US Highway 64.

Flooding developed quickly along the South Toe River, when repeating thunderstorms produced 2 to 3 inches of rain in a short period of time near Celo. Flooding eventually developed downstream along the South Toe near Newdale. Lower Brown Creek also flooded, as did Black Mountain Campground.
A severe thunderstorm dropped golf ball sized hail near Valle Crucis during the afternoon of the 3rd.

This tornado touched down in the Houstonville Rd area and tracked almost due north. Extensive damage was done to a dairy farm in the Houstonville community, with damage to several other agricultural buildings in the area. In the Misty Rd, Anthony Rd area, the roof was torn off a mobile home as well as another home. The tornado continued to track north, crossing into Yadkin County around Tobacco Rd. At least 13 structures were damaged or destroyed in the county. In addition to the structural damage, damage to tobacco and corn crops was extensive. In addition, numerous trees were blown down along highway 21 and Hunting Creek Rd west of the tornado track. It is unclear whether this was a secondary spin-up or straight-line wind.
Trees down near exit 54 off of Interstate 77.
Trees and power lines down.
A few trees reported down in Andrews area.
Severe urban flooding developed in and around Charlotte, with water of 2 to 3 feet reported at some intersections on the southeast side of the city. This required several rescues.

This tornado moved into far southern Rutherford County from Spartanburg County in the Jonas Rd area. The metal roof was torn off a barn just across the state line. Otherwise, damage was mainly confined to snapped off an uprooted trees, some of which fell on homes. Near the end of the track, a mobile home was lifted and dropped 50-100 feet from its original position, resulting in severe damage.
Quite a few trees down in the Boiling Springs area.
Trees and power lines down.
Two flagpoles and several trees blown down.

This tornado touched down near highway 16 just north of the Catawba County line. Two brick homes and a business received severe roof damage in this area, while a mobile home was completely destroyed. The tornado tracked northeast, damaging the roofs of several homes at a mobile home park along Friendship Church Rd before lifting near the intersection of Millersville Rd and Dairy Rd. Numerous trees were also blown down along the tornado path.
Several reports of trees down just south of the Southpark area. One tree fell on a condominium.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A women drowned from rip currents at Carolina Beach.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice storm produced a 1/4 to 1/2 inch coating of ice across Northern North Carolina starting on the morning of the 15th in the west and continuing into the evening. Downed trees, limbs and power lines created power outages across much of the region. Emergency managers estimated that 12,000 people lost power during the storm.  Surry county reported 300 trees downed across the county with 8,000 people losing power in the Dobson, Mount Airy and Westfield areas.

Public reported brief tornado with several trees down near Lagrange.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain quickly transitioned to heavy snow across the higher elevations of the North Carolina mountains during the evening of the 21st. Snow quickly accumulated to depths of 1 to 3 inches, mainly in areas above about 3500 feet. Highway 441 was closed through the Smokies for a brief period of time. Portions of the Blue Ridge Parkway were also reported to be slick and hazardous.  Mainly rain fell in the valleys during this time.
